coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent lignin hybridized carbon quantum dots composites for bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Bailiang
Yang, Yang
Sun, Yongchang
Fan, Jinshuan
Li, Xinping
Zhang, Zhao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable interests because of their unique luminescence properties and promising applications in bioimaging. Lignin hybridized CQDs (L-CQDs) are facilely synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method with different molar ratios of citric acid and ethanediamine. The morphology and chemical structures of L-CQDs are investigated by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, 2D-HSQC NMR spectra. The resultant L-CQDs with near spherical morphology are less than 10 nm in diameter, which are essentially composed of main interunit linkages and aromatic ring structures. The L-CQDs exhibit the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior with emission maximum ranging from 454 to 535 nm under excitation at 375–460 nm. The maximum emission of the L-CQDs red-shift to a longer wavelength with a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelengths are gradually increased. L-CQDs show the low cytotoxicity and good cellular biocompatibility, demonstrating the great potential of the L-CQDs for bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Bioimaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Lignin and its blends, Edited by : Dr. Maurice Collins and Dr. Roberto Rinaldi

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the sample LC-11.
UV–vis absorption spectra of the LC-11, LC-12 and LC-21.
Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of the LC-11(a), LC-12(b), LC-21(c) and fluorescent intensity of LC-11 under different irradiation time.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the LC-11, LC-12 and LC-21 with different excitation wavelengths.
FT-IR spectra of the AL and LC-11.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the LC-11, (b) XPS C 1 s peaks and fitting curves, (c) XPS N 1 s peaks and fitting curves, (d) XPS O 1 s peaks and fitting curves.
Cell viability values (%) estimated by MTT assay after incubation with the sample LC-11 with different concentrations at 37 °C for 24 h.

Bright-field images (left), TP microscope images (middle), and the overlay of fluorescence and bright-field images (right) of HeLa cells incubated with the sample LC-11 (0.5 mg/ mL) for 12 h at 37 °C. CLSM images were taken under different excitation (a) λ = 280 nm, (b) λ = 380 nm, (c) λ = 420 nm, and (d) λ = 500 nm (scale bar = 20 μm).

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent lignin hybridized carbon quantum dots composites for bioimaging applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable interests because of their unique luminescence properties and promising applications in bioimaging. Lignin hybridized CQDs (L-CQDs) are facilely synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method with different molar ratios of citric acid and ethanediamine. The morphology and chemical structures of L-CQDs are investigated by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, 2D-HSQC NMR spectra. The resultant L-CQDs with near spherical morphology are less than 10 nm in diameter, which are essentially composed of main interunit linkages and aromatic ring structures. The L-CQDs exhibit the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior with emission maximum ranging from 454 to 535 nm under excitation at 375–460 nm. The maximum emission of the L-CQDs red-shift to a longer wavelength with a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelengths are gradually increased. L-CQDs show the low cytotoxicity and good cellular biocompatibility, demonstrating the great potential of the L-CQDs for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin as a renewable material is an abundant organic polymer in nature. The lignin polymer is synthesized by peroxidase-mediated dehydrogenation of monolignol units, giving a heterogeneous structure formed by basic units linked by CC bonds and aryl–ether linkages [1,2]. Currently, the major source of lignin is produced in the pulp and paper industry as a waste product, and mostly used as a low value fuel to provide heat and power, resulting in the massive waste of this resource as well as pollution of the environment [3]. Among different sources and pulping processes, various types of lignin could be produced with varying amounts of monomeric units and linkages, such as alkali lignin (AL), Kraft lignin and lignosulfonate [4,5]. To date, various researchers have reported different lignin valorization methods to modify lignin and synthesize lignin-based polymers, such as phenolic resin, epoxy polymer, polyurethane and carbon fiber [4,6–9].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally defined as the interesting class of carbon nanoparticles that are mainly comprised of carbon with a size below 10 nm [10]. Comparing with traditional organic dyes or semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs have many advantages due to their unique and novel properties, including favorable optical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, low cost, easy preparation and environmental friendliness [11–13]. Therefore, great efforts have been focused on the promising preparation of CQDs and developing CQDs in the applications of bioimaging, medical diagnosis, photocatalysis and photovoltaic devices [14–16]. CQDs can be synthesized by “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [17]. Top-down approaches involve arc-discharge, laser ablation, and electrochemical oxidation, where the CQDs are formed or “broken off” from larger carbon material [11]. Bottom-up approaches refer to obtaining CQDs from small organic molecular precursors, including microwave/ultrasonic, plasma and hydrothermal treatment have been developed for the fabrication of CQDs [14,18,19]. Nevertheless, many of these synthesis methods involve expensive or toxic starting materials, high temperature, long reaction time and further surface-passivation [20]. Hydrothermal methods using natural precursors to produce CQDs have been widely reported because of their green chemistry nature [21,22].

CQDs have been synthesized by a lot of precursors used as the starting materials. Typically, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules are selected as the most reliable precursors to form the high quality CQDs [23–25]. However, the toxicity of the aromatic hydrocarbon precursors severely limited their widespread use. Therefore, developing non-toxic precursors for producing the CQDs instead of toxic organic precursors are still desirable. Some effort has been made to utilize natural precursors, including glucose [26], alginate [27], natural products [28] and lignin [29] due to their renewable, nontoxic and biocompatible properties. Lignin, composed of a long hydrophobic backbone branched with hydrophilic side chains (carboxyl, and phenolic hydroxyl groups), and thus possesses a certain degree of surface activity. Recently, Chen et al. [30] have firstly synthesized the CQDs form lignin modified with H2O2 as an oxidizing agent through a hydrothermal method, the resulted CQDs were less efficient for bio-imaging application and also the quantum yields of resulted CQDs as well as the type of lignin precursors used were not mentioned. Ding et al. [31] reported the single-crystalline CQDs from alkali lignin modified with nitric acid via the oxidized cleavage step followed by the hydrothermal method, this procedure used prolong reaction time and high energy consuming procedure (ultrasonication). However, there are few reports about the CQDs derived from AL modified with the citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) for bioimaging applications.

In this work, we presented a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach to synthesize the lignin hybridized CQDs via the lignin-CQDs interactions. The as-prepared L-CQDs exhibited excellent photoluminescence properties, good water solubility, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. The effects of different molar ratios of CA and EDA on their photophysical and chemical properties of the L-CQDs were fully discussed. The L-CQDs was characterized using by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), two-dimensional heteronuclear single-quantum coherence spectroscopy nuclear magnetic resonance (2D-HSQC NMR) and fluorescence spectra. The cytotoxicity of L-CQDs against HeLa cells was studied through standard MTT assay. The L-CQDs served as efficient probes for cellular multicolor imaging were also investigated.

Alkali lignin (AL) (low sulfonation, Mw ∼ 10,000) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich in powder form. Citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China) and used as without further treatment.

L-CQDs were prepared by a facile one pot hydrothermal method, different molar ratios of CA, EDA and AL were prepared according to the formulation listed in Table S1. In a typical synthesis, different complexes were dissolved in a 25 mL beaker with 15 mL DI water. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave, and then heated at 150 °C for 4 h. The solution was purified in a centrifuge for 30 min to remove agglomerated particles, and then the L-CQDs solutions were extensively dialyzed against redistilled water using dialysis tubing (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 2 days. Afterwards, the resulting yellow solution was freeze-dried to obtain the final L-CQDs powder.

FT-IR analysis was accomplished using a Nicolet 6700 Frontier system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., Madison, WI, USA). The spectral width was from 4000 to 800 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. For each sample 32 scans were recorded. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Ltd., UK) using Al Kα source. 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of L-CQDs dissolved in methanol‑d 4 were recorded using a Bruker AVIII NMR spectrometer at 400 MHz (Bruker Corp., Karlsruhe, Germany). The spectra were collected according to the parameters reported previously [32]. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded over the range of 200–800 nm on a Cary 300 UV spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Inc., Livingston, U.K.) equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. The emission and excitation light was scattered and collected in an integrating sphere and detected with a photomultiplier with a detection range of 200–1000 nm. PL decay curves were recorded at room temperature under atmospheric condition using the time-correlated double-photon counting technique for L-CQDs transitions, with the different excitation wavelengths. For measurement of the luminescence quantum yields (QY) of the L-CQDs in PBS solution, the emission spectra were recorded with the different excitation wavelengths, and the integrated areas of the luminescence corrected spectra were measured. The QY was then calculated by comparison with quinine sulfate as reference using the following equation:
ϕ =
I
I R
⋅
A R
A
⋅
n
n R
2
⋅
ϕ R
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area under the luminescence spectra. A is the absorbance, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and R refers to the reference fluorophore, quinine sulfate. Φ
R = 0.56 in 0.1 M sulfuric acid was used as the reference quantum yield.

The methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assays were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the prepared L-CQDs in HeLa cells. Raw HeLa cells (6 × 103 cells per well) were added to a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h under an atmosphere of 5% CO2. Then, L-CQDs with different concentrations (0–1 mg/mL) were introduced to the cells and further incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in the presence of 5% CO2. After that, the cells were rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4) and treated with MTT solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL), and then incubated for another 4 h. Finally, the optical absorbance at 570 nm of each well was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad) to calculate the cell viability. The cell viabilities values were calculated according to the following formula: cell viability (%) = (the absorbance of the experimental group/the absorbance of the control group) × 100%.

Cellular imaging was evaluated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). HeLa cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 6-well culture plates for 12 h. L-CQDs with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL were added to the cells at 37 °C for 1 h. After that, the culture medium was carefully removed and the cells were thoroughly rinsed three times with PBS and kept in PBS for imaging measurement. Subsequently, the cells were examined by CLSM (Zeiss Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope; LSM7 DUO) using ZEN 2009 software (Carl Zeiss).

The preparation of L-CQDs was facilely synthesized by employing the AL as the raw material with different molar ratios of CA and EDA via one-pot hydrothermal method. The photographs of the L-CQDs presented translucent and homogeneous (Fig. S1), a color change (yellow-brownish) was observed when the molar ratios of CA and EDA was increased in the L-CQDs. The yellow-brownish color probably originated from the essential color of AL and/or CQDs. The morphology of the L-CQDs (LC-11) was presented by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the LC-11 was well dispersed in water and showed obvious lattice fringes, which displayed monodisperse nanoparticles of near spherical morphology below 10 nm. Because the AL has an amorphous structure, the lattice fringes are most likely caused by the ordered arrangement imposed by π − π aggregation [33].

The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of the L-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the UV–vis absorption spectra of the L-CQDs exhibited a well-defined absorption band at 280 nm attributed to the structure of soluble AL, and a relatively narrow peak at 300–400 nm. The results were probably assigned to the typical absorptions of aromatic π systems and n − p* transitions of carbonyl and other oxygen-containing groups [34]. The excitation and emission FL spectra of the L-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 . The maximum emission intensity of the sample LC-11 was located at 454 nm when excited at 377 nm. As confirmed, the as-prepared LC-11 exhibited very high fluorescence stability in a prolonged irradiation time. The sample LC-12 showed the maximum emission peak at 535 nm upon excitation at 460 nm, while the sample LC-21 displayed the maximum emission position at 465 nm corresponding to the excitation at 375 nm. It is obvious that the sample LC-11 demonstrated the highest photoluminescent intensity among the same concentration of the L-CQDs with different molar ratios of CA and EDA (Fig. S2). These different fluorescence intensities were probably due to the chemical structure of CQDs, most of which have no graphite lattices [12]. The fluorescence emission intensities of the surface state/molecule state of CQDs were also strongly affected by surrounding solvents under exposure to high-power UV radiation [35]. Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of LC-11 in a prolonged irradiation time have been measured as shown in Fig. 3d, the result indicated that the as-prepared lignin CQDs exhibited very high fluorescence stability in a prolonged irradiation time. Additionally, the quantum yields (QY) of the LC-11 at the different excitation wavelengths were listed in Table S2. The QY of the LC-11 at 380 nm excitation was calculated to be 43%. The typical fluorescence decay curves for the LC-11 irradiated at the different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3. These curves can be fitted to a double-exponential function as I(t) = B1e (−t/τ1) + B2e (−t/τ2), where B1, B2 are fractional intensities, τ1 and τ2 are fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime of the sample LC-11 at the different excitation wavelengths was approximately 10 ns.

Because the L-CQDs can absorb the energy over a broad area, the emission FL spectra of L-CQDs obtained using different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 4 . The emission peak was shifted from 445 to 510 nm in the sample LC-11 as the excitation wavelength was increased from 330 to 410 nm (Fig. 4a). When the excitation wavelength was increased from 330 to 380 nm, the emission peak became obviously stronger and slightly red-shift. When the excitation wavelength increased to 410 nm from 380, the emission peak presented red-shift with remarkably decreased intensities. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 4b, the emission peak of the sample LC-12 varied from 540 to 554 nm with a gradual decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength increased from 470 to 590 nm. As for the sample LC-21 (Fig. 4c), when the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 400 nm, the same tendency of the emission change in the sample LC-21 as those in the sample LC-11 except for its maximum emission peak at 468 nm with excitation wavelength at 380 nm. It is obvious that the FL spectra of L-CQDs show a typical excitation wavelength-dependent feature. These phenomenons have been reported previously with other CQDs [22,33]. Moreover, all the emission spectra also exhibit the characteristic feature that the maximum emission red shifted with a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelength are gradually increased. The tunable emissions are probably ascribed to the different sizes of L-CQDs or the different distribution of emissive energy traps on the surface of L-CQDs [36,37]. However, up to date, the exact mechanism of the excitation-dependent FL behavior in L-CQDs remains unclear. These tunable fluorescence emission properties of as-prepared L-CQDs are particular attractive for sensing, bioimaging and synthesizing novel photocatalysts [36,37].

The sample LC-11 was selected for further chemical characterization due to its highest fluorescence intensity among different L-CQDs. The surface groups of the AL and LC-11 were compared using FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 , the broad peak at 3448 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH groups, the peaks at 2945 and 2943 cm−1 are assigned to the CH stretching of methyl or methylene groups. The three peaks at 1606, 1506, and 1412 cm−1 are associated with stretching vibrations of CC bonds in the aromatic skeleton while the peaks at 1212 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of aromatic CO bonds, which are attributed to the characteristic absorptions of AL. As compared to AL, the characteristic peak of lignin at 1606 cm−1 also exists in the sample LC-11. Moreover, the new peak at 1708 cm−1 corresponding to the CO vibration of hydrogen-bonded carboxyl groups appears in the LC-11 whereas no peak at 1736 cm−1 ascribed to the CO vibration of free carboxyl groups exist, suggesting that the formation of hydrogen bonds is partially existed between polar groups in the AL and CQDs [10,38]. Furthermore, the band at 1579 cm−1 is assigned to the deformation vibrations of NH in the sample LC-11, the CONR vibration at 1647 cm−1 is located to the acylation reaction, indicating that the formation of CONR bonds between polar groups in the AL and CQDs are partially appeared [39]. Additionally, it is noted the spectral assignments at 869, 1035 and 1139 cm−1 arising from aromatic units of the lignin simultaneously present in AL and LC-11 [40,41], these signals revealed that the L-CQDs contain the aromatic structures of AL, which was further confirmed by the following 2D-HSQC NMR spectra.

The elemental composition and chemical bonding configurations of the sample LC-11 were determined by XPS. As shown in Fig. 6 , the XPS survey spectrum shows that the sample LC-11 is mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum could be divided into three peaks at 284.3, 285.6, and 288.2 eV, which can be attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO/CN, respectively. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 531.3, 532.4, and 533.3 eV corresponding to OCO/ArOAr, CO/COC, and ArOH bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum suggests the presence of pyridinic N (397.8 eV), pyrrolic N (399.2 eV) and quaternary N (400.7 eV). The results of XPS analysis confirm that the L-CQDs contain aromatic structures and the presence of CO and NH in the L-CQDs, which is in good agreement with the FT-IR spectra.

2D-HSQC NMR spectra of the sample LC-11 are shown in Fig. S4. The cross-signals of different interunit linkages in the side-chain region (δC/δH 50.0–90.0/2.50–6.00 ppm) are depicted in Fig. S4a. The cross-signals from lignin aromatic rings in the aromatic region (δC/δH 100.0–135.0/6.00–8.00 ppm) are observed in Fig. S4b. The corresponding main substructures are shown in Fig. S5. The assignments of cross-signals in the 2D-HSQC NMR spectra are listed in Table S3 according to the previous publications [32]. In the side-chain region, the cross-peaks of methoxy groups (-OCH3, δC/δH 55.6/3.75 ppm) and β-O-4 substructures were the most predominant. Moreover, the resinol substructures (β-β, α-O-γ, and γ-O-α linkages, B) and phenylcoumaran units (β-5 and α-O-4 linkages, C) were observed in the spectra. In the aromatic regions, the cross-signals corresponding to the aromatic rings of syringyl (S/S′) and guaiacyl (G) lignin units were identified in the spectra. The S units showed a prominent signal for the C2,6-H2,6 correlations appear at δC/δH 103.5/6.65 ppm. By contrast, the different correlations of C2-H2 (δC/δH 110.4/6.95 ppm), C5-H5 (δC/δH 115.0/6.72 ppm), C6-H6 (δC/δH 118.5/6.80 ppm) are shown in the G units. The results suggested that main interunit linkages and aromatic ring structures of the AL are present in the L-CQDs.

In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity and interaction with cells of the L-CQDs, the standard MTT assay were carried out using HeLa cells as the model. The cell viabilities were examined upon exposure to the different concentrations of the sample LC-11 (0, 0.031, 0.062, 0.125, 0.25, 0.5 and 1 mg/mL). As shown in Fig. 7 , compared with the control sample without the L-CQDs, it is obvious that the cell viabilities values were gradually increased with the decreasing concentration of the LC-11. The cell viabilities were greater than 90% at the concentration of 0.062 mg/mL and 0.031 mg/mL after 24 h incubation. Further increasing the concentration of the LC-11 from 0.125 mg/mL to 0.5 mg/mL, their viabilities of cells was still over 80%. The results demonstrated that the LC-11 had excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity towards HeLa cells, which was probably assigned to the low toxicity of CQDs. The low toxicity of L-CQDs indicated the promising potential for bioimaging application.

To investigate the feasibility of L-CQDs as fluorescent probes, the distribution of the sample LC-11 in HeLa cells were visualized by CLSM. CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated with 0.5 mg/mL of the sample LC-11 were used to monitor cellular uptake. As shown in Fig. 8 , the HeLa cells incubated with the sample LC-11 become quite bright owing to the strong fluorescence emitting, which were probably due to the large amount of nanoparticles from the L-CQDs were proposed to be internalized into the cells through endocytosis [42]. It is obvious that the fluorescence brightness of the sample LC-11 within the cells was mainly concentrated on the cell cytoplasm, and very weak emission signals were observed to distribute on the cell nucleus, which were in accordance with the previous literatures that CQDs were able to stain both the cytoplasm and cell membrane but difficult to label the nucleus [22]. The excitation-dependent PL behavior of the sample LC-11 exhibited blue (Fig. 8b), green (Fig. 8c), red (Fig. 8d), corresponding to three different colors at excitation wavelengths of 380 nm, 420 nm and 500 nm, respectively. Thus, the LC-11 fabricated shows multicolor fluorescent emission under different laser excitation, which endows the L-CQDs with an intrinsic ability to demonstrate the great potential in bioimaging and biolabeling fields.

In summary, we have presented one-pot hydrothermal method to facilely synthesize the L-CQDs with the AL in the presence of CA and EDA. The newly synthesized L-CQDs are well dispersed in water, and display near spherical morphology with diameter less than 10 nm. L-CQDs are essentially composed of main interunit linkages and aromatic ring structures. The L-CQDs exhibit the excitation-dependent FL behavior with emission maximum ranging from 454 to 535 nm under 375–460 nm excitation. The L-CQDs with equal molar ratio of CA and EDA display the highest FL intensity at the same concentration. The L-CQDs show multicolor fluorescent emission under different excitation wavelength and the maximum emission of the L-CQDs red-shift to a longer wavelength with a decrease in intensity as the excitation wavelengths are gradually increased. The MTT assay against HeLa cells verified the low cytotoxicity of the L-CQDs. The multicolor fluorescence of the L-CQDs could be easily internalized by HeLa cells, demonstrating the great potential applications in bioimaging and biolabeling fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude for the grants from the National Key Research and Development Program of China(2017YFB0307903), Natural Science Foundation of China (21706154), Doctoral Scientific Research Fund by Shaanxi University of Science & Technology (BJ15-26), and Shaanxi Province Key Laboratory of Exploitation and Utilization of Economic Plant Resources (zuly1701).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.11.018.
